summary: This session simulates a technical interview for a senior software engineering role, focusing on data structures and algorithms. The interviewer, Sarf, and candidate, Pratik, discuss fundamental concepts like LinkedList vs. ArrayList, HashMap internals, and sorting algorithms (Merge Sort, Quick Sort). The interview then progresses to problem-solving, where Pratik designs and implements a leaderboard system, addressing optimization, edge cases, and production considerations like concurrency. Finally, Pratik solves the 'largest sum subarray' problem using Kadane's algorithm. The session concludes with constructive feedback from both participants.

turns.speaker: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Hi everyone, my name is Roman Zki and I'm a product manager at EPAM Anx. I'm also the host of today's webinar. Today, we'll be joined by two speakers, Sarf and Pratik, and we'll be talking about all things data structures and algorithms for an interview for a position with four plus years of experience. I believe it's going to be a really incredible session to get some hands-on insights on what a real-life interview looks like.

turns.speaker: UNKNOWN
turns.text: This video is brought to you with the support of Anx. Anx is a program developed by EPAM, aimed at enhancing and propagating best software engineering practices and processes across the organization and all team levels. It offers a suite of services, tools, and educational programs to continuously improve software development performance, smoothly integrate GNI tools into everyday work, and foster a culture of excellence and innovation among engineering teams. If you enjoy this video, be sure to check out the links in the description and explore more about what Anx has to offer on engineering excellence for your teams. So now, I'd like to give a couple of words to introduce yourself first. For the interviewer, Sarf, the stage is yours.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Hi, hello everyone. My name is Sarf. I'm a lead engineer at EPAM Systems. I have about 14 years of experience in the IT industry. Throughout my journey, I was involved in product development and also worked on Java DSA and system design aspects. Today, I'll be playing the role of the interviewer, conducting the interview on Java DSA.

turns.speaker: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Wow, thanks. Such a cool experience. You're such a cool guy, and thank you for joining us today. Now, our candidate, Pratik, the word is for you.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Hi everyone. I'm Pratik Jaab, and I'm from the EPAM Systems team. I'm playing the role of the candidate. Regarding my background, I have nearly seven years of experience in backend development, specifically with Java, DSA, and system design.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Thank you for the introduction. It seems that we are now all set up, so let's jump into the interview process. There we go. The format of this interview will be like this: I'll be asking some theoretical questions, and then we'll go into problem-solving. The idea is for you to tell me about the requirements, the scope, what kind of data structure you'll be using, then go into the edge cases, and then implement it.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, so can you please tell me what is the difference between LinkedList and ArrayList?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay. So, LinkedList basically works on address location and the node part. It stores two things: actual data and the address of the next data. The ArrayList stores data in an actual array internally. If you talk about LinkedList in Java, it uses a doubly LinkedList internally. Basically, it points to the next location of the data as well as the previous location of the data.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: And how does memory allocation happen with respect to static versus dynamic? How is it different in ArrayList and how is it different in LinkedList?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: In an ArrayList, it stores the data in continuous memory locations. So, if one element is stored at one location, the exact next location will store the second element. But in a LinkedList, it stores at different memory locations. When it points to the next element, it stores the address of the next memory location into the first node of that particular LinkedList. So, the location is continuous in an ArrayList, but not continuous in a LinkedList. That's why LinkedList is dynamic.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, so let's say if I have a constraint on space, if I have to choose between LinkedList and ArrayList, what kind of data structure would I go with?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I would suggest that if there are constraints in the memory location, then we should go with the LinkedList. Because, according to the structure of the LinkedList, it stores the data at random spaces. So, we can use whichever available space in our memory efficiently by spreading the data and storing its memory location into the current node. That's how we should choose the LinkedList over there.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay. Can you please tell me how the internals of HashMap look like?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay. Internally, the HashMap stores key and value for our usage. But internally, it resolves collisions. Before Java 8, it stored them in a LinkedList, specifically a doubly LinkedList. But in Java 8, it stores them in a binary search tree, where each key will be stored in the binary tree. So, whenever we get a particular element in the HashMap, it searches using the binary search algorithm, which gives us more efficiency compared to the LinkedList.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: So, let's say if a collision happens with the key, how does it resolve that? What kind of techniques does it use?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: There are multiple techniques we can use for resolving collisions. Probing is one of the techniques currently used in HashMap. What it does is, it internally calls the hash method, which creates a hash code. According to the hash code, it stores the data. So, whenever a collision happens, it calls the hash method, which creates a hash code, and our data will be stored at a different location for that hash. So, linear probing and cubic probing are different methods we can use if a collision happens.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: You mentioned the LinkedList is being stored, but how does it guarantee that if we have a large set of data, how does it resolve that? Let's say if you have a key with multiple collisions, and you are storing that value. Now, how do you resolve it in a faster way to provide access to the values for that particular key? For example, if you have a key 'XYZ' and corresponding values, and the same key can have multiple values, how can I fetch those in a LinkedList manner?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay. Whenever we have the same key, and let's say the hash code is also calculated to be the same in the hash method, it stores them in a LinkedList in a linear manner. First, it will fetch the full LinkedList for that particular key, and then it will traverse one by one. Once it reaches the particular element we want to fetch, it will return the value of that element. But in that case, the complexity will be O(N).

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: But if I have to optimize it, how can I do that?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: That's why before Java 8, it used a LinkedList for storing the value. But in Java 8, it was improved. Whenever we have a large list, it converts to a binary search tree. And whenever we want to search a node, it searches within that list using the binary search algorithm. When it starts searching, it gives us less time complexity. In a LinkedList, it gives O(N), but in a binary search tree, it gives O(log N). That's why in Java 8, it converted to a binary search tree.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: And how are Merge Sort and Quick Sort different? Which one is better?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: We cannot say one is better, but in different scenarios, we can use different algorithms. Let me give a scenario for Quick Sort. Let's say we have a very large number of data sets. Quick Sort divides and then conquers the data. It divides on the basis of one pivot element. This pivot element is decided based on the data; it can be a middle or a lesser element. According to this pivot element, it divides on the basis of smaller elements and larger elements. Then it divides till the end of the list and then conquers by sorting it and merging the data into a single sorted array. Why is Quick Sort not suitable for small data sets? Because we are dividing and then conquering the array, this becomes a heavy operation if we use it for small data sets, because for each and every element, we are dividing and then conquering the data. But in Merge Sort, we cannot say it's good for large data sets, but for small data sets, Merge Sort will be used because it is merging at each and every element. It calculates if it's lesser, it puts the data; if it's greater, it rejects and puts data from a different data set. Merge Sort creates subsets, and Quick Sort creates data sets on the basis of the pivot element.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: So, if we consider the complexity, what is the complexity in the worst-case scenario in both cases?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: For Quick Sort, it gives O(N log N), and the same for Merge Sort also, but I'm not sure about Merge Sort.

For `increaseScoreByOne`, you'll be given a player ID, and you have to increment the current score by one. Similarly, for `decreaseScoreByOne`, the current score will be decreased by one. `getLeader` should return the player ID with the max score. At any point, these three methods will be called. As a service provider, you have to come up with this implementation. As part of this assessment, I'm looking for what kind of data structure you'll be using, what will be the complexity of these three methods, and also identifying the edge cases for these three methods, which you'll be implementing. Your code should be functional.

training_pairs.question: Can you please tell me what is the difference between LinkedList and ArrayList?
training_pairs.answer: LinkedList basically works on address location and the node part. It stores actual data and the address of the next data. ArrayList stores data in an actual array internally. In Java, LinkedList uses a doubly LinkedList internally, pointing to both next and previous data locations.
training_pairs.follow_up: How does memory allocation happen with respect to static versus dynamic? How is it different in ArrayList and how is it different in LinkedList?

training_pairs.question: If I have a constraint on space, if I have to choose between LinkedList and ArrayList, what kind of data structure would I go with?
training_pairs.answer: If there are constraints in memory location, we should go with the LinkedList. Because, according to the structure of the LinkedList, it stores the data at random spaces. So, we can use whichever available space in our memory efficiently by spreading the data and storing its memory location into the current node. That's how we should choose the LinkedList over there.

training_pairs.question: Can you please tell me how the internals of HashMap look like?
training_pairs.answer: Internally, the HashMap stores key and value. It resolves collisions; before Java 8, it used a doubly LinkedList, but in Java 8, it uses a binary search tree where each key is stored. This allows searching using the binary search algorithm, which is more efficient.
training_pairs.follow_up: So, let's say if a collision happens with the key, how does it resolve that? What kind of techniques does it use?

training_pairs.question: You mentioned the LinkedList is being stored, but how does it guarantee that if we have a large set of data, how does it resolve that? Let's say if you have a key with multiple collisions, and you are storing that value. Now, how do you resolve it in a faster way to provide access to the values for that particular key? For example, if you have a key 'XYZ' and corresponding values, and the same key can have multiple values, how can I fetch those in a LinkedList manner?
training_pairs.answer: Whenever we have the same key and the hash code is also the same, it stores them in a LinkedList linearly. It fetches the full LinkedList for that key and then traverses one by one to find the element, resulting in O(N) complexity. To optimize this, Java 8 improved it by converting large lists to a binary search tree, allowing searches in O(log N) time.

training_pairs.question: How are Merge Sort and Quick Sort different? Which one is better?
training_pairs.answer: One is not inherently better; they suit different scenarios. Quick Sort is for very large datasets, using a divide-and-conquer approach with a pivot element. It's not suitable for small datasets due to heavy division operations. Merge Sort is better for small datasets, merging subsets by calculating and placing elements. Quick Sort creates data sets based on a pivot, while Merge Sort creates subsets.
training_pairs.follow_up: So, if we consider the complexity, what is the complexity in the worst-case scenario in both cases?

training_pairs.question: Design and implement a leaderboard with three methods: `increaseScoreByOne`, `decreaseScoreByOne`, and `getLeader`. `increaseScoreByOne` increments a player's score by one, `decreaseScoreByOne` decrements it by one, and `getLeader` returns the player ID with the maximum score. Your implementation should consider data structure choice, complexity, edge cases, and functional code. The focus should be on time complexity first.
training_pairs.answer: Initially, I would use a `HashMap<PlayerID, Score>` for O(1) `increase` and `decrease` operations. For `getLeader`, I would sort the map by value, which would be O(N log N), or traverse it, which is O(N). However, for better performance, I would use a `TreeMap<Score, HashSet<PlayerID>>` where the score is the key (allowing automatic sorting) and a HashSet stores player IDs for players with the same score. This would make `increase` and `decrease` O(log N) and `getLeader` O(1) by fetching the last entry.
training_pairs.follow_up: What kind of challenges do you see with respect to concurrency? What are the challenges coming as part of your concurrency?

training_pairs.question: What kind of consistency issue do you see when multiple threads access `increaseScore` and `decreaseScore` methods, and `getLeader` is called in between?
training_pairs.answer: If one thread increases a score and another decreases it, and `getLeader` is called concurrently, we could get a false or wrong leader. This is because the leaderboard map and sorted data map might not be updated synchronously across threads, leading to inconsistent data being returned.
training_pairs.follow_up: So, how do you resolve this concurrency issue? What are the ways you can mitigate that?

training_pairs.question: How can you resolve concurrency issues in the leaderboard design without compromising performance, especially when the score is frequently changing?
training_pairs.answer: We can use concurrent collection frameworks for the map and TreeMap. For thread-safe score updates without blocking the entire method, we can use `AtomicInteger` for the score itself. This ensures that when updating the score, it fetches the latest value and updates it atomically, improving performance compared to broad synchronization blocks.

training_pairs.question: Given an integer array, find the subarray with the largest sum. The subarray has to be continuous. For example, for [4, -1, 2, 1], the largest sum is 6.
training_pairs.answer: This problem can be solved using Kadane's algorithm. It calculates the maximum sum of a subarray by iterating through the array. It maintains a `currentMax` sum, adding elements to it. If `currentMax` becomes negative, it resets to zero, as a negative sum would only decrease the total. It also keeps track of a global `maxSum` found so far.
training_pairs.follow_up: So, what is the advantage of using Kadane's algorithm here?

training_pairs.question: What is the advantage of using Kadane's algorithm for finding the largest sum subarray?
training_pairs.answer: The main advantage is its time complexity. Kadane's algorithm solves the problem in O(N) time, which is significantly more efficient than a brute-force approach that would involve nested loops and result in O(N^2) complexity. It effectively improves both time and space complexity by making a single pass through the array.
